content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission


May 28, 1992


Sydney M. Irmas
East Tower
11835 W. Olympic Blvd., Suite 1160
Los Angeles, CA  90064

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-92-199

Dear Mr. Irmas:

You have requested advice concerning the conflict of interest
disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act.")1/

QUESTIONS

1. Must you report on your statement of economic interests
(Form 730) property owned and income received by a professional
corporation of which you are the sole shareholder?

2. Must you report property owned and income received by the
pension trust for your corporation of which you are the sole
trustee and sole employee? If so, must you report real property
given as security when the pension trust lends money and in return
receives as collateral notes and deeds of trust?

3. Must you report income received by the family intervivos
revocable trust which holds all other assets held by you and your
spouse for which you are a co-trustee?


1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Sydney M. Irmas
Our File No. A-92-199
Page 2


CONCLUSIONS

1. If your professional corporation is located in or does
business in the jurisdiction of the Housing Commission of Los
Angeles County and its value is $1,000 or more, you must report
the corporation as an investment on Schedule A of Form 730. The
combined gross income received by the corporation and the gross
income you are entitled to receive from the corporation must be
reported on Schedule D if $250 or more is received during a
reporting period. Your position with the corporation must be
disclosed on Schedule G. Because you own a 10 percent or greater
interest in the corporation, you also must disclose interests in
real property and investments with a value of $1,000 or more which
are held by the corporation, as well as sources of income totaling
$10,000 or more to the corporation if such assets and sources of
income are located in or doing business in the jurisdiction of the
Housing Commission. Such interests in real property, investments,
and sources of income are disclosed on Schedules C-1, C-2 and H-2
of Form 730, respectively.

2. If the pension trust qualifies under Internal Revenue
Code Section 401(a), the trust and any income from or assets held
by the trust are excluded from disclosure. However, if the trust
does not qualify under Internal Revenue Code Section 401(a) and
the trust is operated for profit, the trust will be deemed a
business entity and the same disclosure requirements will be
applicable to the trust as are outlined above for the corporation.
Deeds of trust or other assets held by the pension trust as
security for loans made by the trust are reportable if the trust
is reportable.

3. Income received by the intervivos trust is reportable on
Schedule D if your pro rata share of the income amounts to $250 or
more during the reporting period. Interests in real property and
investments with an individual value of $1,000 or more which are
held by this trust must be disclosed on Schedule C-1 and Schedule
C-2, respectively, if the interests in real property are located
in your jurisdiction or the investments are located in or do
business in your jurisdiction.

FACTS

You are the sole shareholder of a professional corporation.
The professional corporation has a pension trust in which you are
the sole trustee and employee. All other assets held by you and
your spouse are held in a family intervivos revocable trust for
which you are a co-trustee. As a newly appointed member of the
Housing Commission of Los Angeles County, you must disclose all
investments in entities located in or doing business in the
jurisdiction of the Housing Commission, interests in real property
located in the Commission's jurisdiction, and income received from
sources located in or doing business in the jurisdiction.

' Sydney M. Irmas
Our File No. A-92-199
Page 3

ANALYSIS

The Act requires state and local government officials to file
periodic reports disclosing their personal financial interests,
including investments in business entities and interests in real
property with an individual value of $1,000 or more, and sources
of income of $250 or more received during a reporting period.
(Section 87200, et seq.)

The term "business entity" is defined in Section 82005 to
mean:

...any organization or enterprise operated for profit,
including but not limited to a proprietorship,
partnership, firm, business trust, joint venture,
syndicate, corporation or association.

The terms "interest in real property" and "investment" are
defined in Sections 82033 and 82034, respectively, to include
assets owned directly, indirectly, or beneficially by the filer or
by the filer's spouse or dependent children, as well as the pro
rata share of assets held by any business entity or trust in which
the official owns a 10 percent or greater interest.

The term "income" is defined in Section 82030 to include a
pro rata share of any income of any business entity or trust in
which the official or his or her spouse owns, directly,
indirectly, or beneficially, a 10 percent or greater interest.
However, with regard to pension plans, Section 82030 excludes
income from a defined benefit pension plan qualified under
Internal Revenue Code Section 401(a). Accordingly, assets held by
such a pension plan also are not reportable.

With regard to disclosing interests held and income received
by a trust, an official has a direct, indirect or beneficial
interest in a trust if the filer is:

(1) A maker and:
(A) Can revoke or terminate the trust;
(B) Has retained or reserved any rights to
the income or principal of the trust or retained any
reversionary or remainder interest; or
(C) Has retained or reserved any power of
appointment, including but not limited to the power to
amend, alter or designate, either alone or in
conjunction with anyone else, the person or persons who
shall possess or enjoy the property or income therefrom.
(2) A beneficiary and:
(A) Presently receives income; or
(B) Has an irrevocable future right to
receive income or principal....

Regulation 18234(c).

Sydney M. Irmas
Our File No. A-92-199
Page 4

Disclosure under the Act is limited to real property which is
located in the official's jurisdiction, and to business entities
and sources of income which are located in or doing business in
the official's jurisdiction. Section 82035 defines "jurisdiction"
as:

... with respect to a local government agency, the
region, county, city, district or other geographical
area in which it has jurisdiction.... Real property
shall be deemed to be "within the jurisdiction" with
respect to a local government agency if the property or
any part of it is located within or not more than two
miles outside the boundaries of the jurisdiction or
within two miles of any land owned or used by the local
government agency.

A business entity or source of income is located in or doing
business in the jurisdiction if it or a parent, subsidiary, or a
related business entity:

°

Manufactures, distributes, sells or purchases products
or services on a regular basis in the jurisdiction;

°

Plans to do business in the jurisdiction;

°

Has done business in the jurisdiction within the
previous two years;

°

Has an interest in real property in the jurisdiction; or

°

Has an office in the jurisdiction.

If I can be of further assistance, please contact me at
(916) 322-5662.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Carla Wardlow
Acting Division Chief
Technical Assistance and
Analysis Division
